---
title: "FHIR: Resource Model"
lang: en-GB
---

## Historical Context — Why FHIR {#sec-fhir-contexto}

### HL7 v3 and the Failed `RIM` Attempt

In the years following the publication of `HL7` v2, it became clear that
the protocol had structural limitations that were difficult to overcome:
the absence of formal semantics, implementation ambiguity, and tight
coupling between pairs of systems. To address these issues, the HL7
International consortium launched, in 2003, **HL7 v3** — a complete
rewrite based on a central information model called `RIM` (Reference
Information Model), a single formal `UML` (Unified Modeling Language)
model designed to represent *all* health information [@Heitmann2004].

The ambition was considerable: a single unified model would unify all
healthcare communication, eliminating the structural ambiguity of `HL7`
v2. In practice, the result was the opposite. The `RIM` was extremely
abstract — hundreds of classes organised in multiple hierarchies — and
its learning curve required months for implementers. `HL7` v3 messages
could reach hundreds of kilobytes for a simple transaction, transported
in `SOAP` (Simple Object Access Protocol) *envelopes* over `XML`
(Extensible Markup Language) Web Services. The entire architecture
required specialised *middleware* — integration engines such as Mirth
Connect or Rhapsody — making installation and maintenance costly and
technically demanding [@BensonGrieve2021].

Adoption fell far short of expectations: `HL7` v2, despite its
limitations, remained dominant. `HL7` v3 was largely confined to paper.

### `CDA` — the Only HL7 v3 Product with Real Adoption

The exception was `CDA` (Clinical Document Architecture), published in
2000 and revised in version R2 in 2005. `CDA` uses the `RIM`, but applies
it to a restricted and concrete domain: clinical documents — discharge
notes, clinical summaries, consultation reports [@HL7_CDA2005].

The structure of a `CDA` document is `XML` with a mandatory header
(identifier, date, patient, author) and a *body* with sections coded in
`LOINC` (Logical Observation Identifiers Names and Codes). The document
is readable by both machine and human. This duality was one of the reasons
for its adoption.

```xml
<!-- CDA R2 excerpt — clinical document header -->
<ClinicalDocument>
  <typeId root="2.16.840.1.113883.1.3" extension="POCD_HD000040"/>
  <templateId root="2.16.840.1.113883.10.20.22.1.1"/>
  <id root="a6e29b60-4d4a-11ed" extension="doc-001"/>
  <code code="34133-9" displayName="Sumário Clínico"/>
  <recordTarget>
    <patientRole>
      <patient>
        <name>
          <given>Maria</given>
          <family>Silva</family>
        </name>
      </patient>
    </patientRole>
  </recordTarget>
</ClinicalDocument>
```

In Europe, the **ELGA** (*Elektronische Gesundheitsakte* — Austrian
Electronic Health Record) programme adopted `CDA` R2 as the base format
for clinical document exchange, becoming one of the most notable European
cases of production deployment [@HL7_CDA2005].

However, `CDA` has clear limits: it is suitable for documents, not for
the granular exchange of clinical data. Sending a laboratory result, a
blood pressure observation, or a medication record via `CDA` would require
wrapping them in a document when the goal is simply to transmit an atomic
datum. This problem would remain unresolved until the emergence of `FHIR`.

### Grahame Grieve and the *Fresh Look* (2011)

In 2011, Grahame Grieve, a New Zealand health interoperability consultant,
published a series of blog posts entitled *Fresh Look at HL7*
[@Grieve2011]. The central thesis was simple: instead of building further
abstractions on top of the `RIM`, it was necessary to start afresh with
the standards of the modern web — `REST` and `JSON`.

The four founding ideas of the *Fresh Look* were:

**1. Atomic resources** — each clinical concept (patient, observation,
medication, clinical encounter) is an independent resource with its own
identifier, transmissible autonomously. There are no monolithic documents
wrapping everything.

**2. `REST` as transport** — instead of `SOAP` *envelopes* and mandatory
*middleware*, any `HTTP` (HyperText Transfer Protocol) server can serve
clinical resources. A `GET /Patient/123` request returns a patient in
`JSON`. The barrier to entry drops drastically.

**3. `JSON` and `XML`** — native support for both formats. Web developers
adopt immediately with `JSON`; legacy systems that prefer `XML` are equally
supported.

**4. The 80/20 rule** — covering 80% of use cases with simple
specifications in the *core* of the standard. The remaining 20% — local
or specialised cases — are resolved with *profiles* and extensions,
without burdening the base model.

The proposal was presented to HL7 International, which adopted it as an
official project. The first draft for experimental use, `DSTU 1` (*Draft
Standard for Trial Use*), was published in 2012. The maturation process
took less than a decade:

| Version | Year | Milestone |
|---------|------|-----------|
| `DSTU 1` | 2012 | First implementers test the specification |
| `DSTU 2` | 2015 | Basis for `SMART on FHIR` (*Substitutable Medical Applications, Reusable Technologies*); first real clinical implementations |
| `STU 3` | 2017 | `STU` (Standard for Trial Use) — stabilisation of *core* resources; broad adoption |
| `R4` | 2019 | First normative version (no longer "trial use") |
| `R5` | 2023 | Technical maturity; alignment with `IPS` (International Patient Summary) |

: FHIR chronological timeline — from DSTU1 to R5 [@HL7_FHIR_R4; @HL7_FHIR_R5]. {#tbl-fhir-timeline}

### Global Regulatory Adoption

The speed of `FHIR` adoption is inseparable from the regulatory
framework. Two milestones are particularly relevant.

In Europe, the `EHDS` (European Health Data Space), a regulation adopted
between 2022 and 2024, designates `FHIR` as the reference format for
the cross-border sharing of health data between Member States. The
**MyHealth@EU** programme, which aims to ensure continuity of care for
European citizens travelling abroad, is built upon this framework.

In the United States, federal legislation from 2016 (known as the *21st
Century Cures Act*) mandated that any `EHR` (Electronic Health Record)
system certified by the `ONC` (Office of the National Coordinator for
Health Information Technology) expose an `API` (Application Programming
Interface) based on `FHIR` R4 accessible to third-party applications,
while simultaneously prohibiting *information blocking* practices —
deliberate restrictions on patient and professional access to their own
data [@ONC2020].

Adoption has also extended to Australia (`ADHA` — Australian Digital
Health Agency) and Canada, making `FHIR` R4 the global reference version.

::: {.callout-note}
**Note** — `SMART on FHIR` is an authorisation and authentication
framework that allows external applications to access `FHIR` data in a
secure and standardised manner, using OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect. It is
the mechanism that enables a health application on a mobile device to
request authorisation from the hospital system to access a patient's
`FHIR` data without knowing the internal details of that system
[@Mandl2020].
:::

## The `FHIR` Resource Model {#sec-fhir-recursos}

### What a *Resource* Is

The fundamental unit of information in `FHIR` is the `Resource`. A
`Resource` represents a discrete clinical or administrative concept —
patient, observation, medication, clinical encounter — that is
independently identifiable, persistible on a server, and transmissible
in a single `HTTP` response.

This atomicity is the essential difference from `CDA`. In a `CDA`
document, the patient, author, observations, and diagnoses all exist
within a single monolithic document. In `FHIR`, each of these concepts
is a separate resource, with its own `URL`, which can be created, read,
updated, and deleted independently.

Every `Resource` has a common anatomy:

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `resourceType` | `string` | Resource type — mandatory. E.g.: `"Patient"`, `"Observation"` |
| `id` | `id` | Unique identifier on the server; appears in the `URL` |
| `meta` | `Meta` | Metadata: version, date of last update, profile followed |
| `implicitRules` | `uri` | `URI` of additional rules; systems that do not know them cannot safely process the resource |
| `language` | `code` | Language of the content; follows `BCP-47` (Best Current Practice 47 — the language tag standard: `pt`, `en`, `de`, `fr`) |
| `text` | `Narrative` | Human-readable `XHTML` (Extensible HyperText Markup Language) narrative; generated automatically by servers |
| `contained` | `Resource[]` | Resources embedded within this resource |
| `extension` | `Extension[]` | Additional fields not defined in the `FHIR` *core* |
| Type-specific elements | — | Fields specific to the resource type: `name`, `birthDate` (in `Patient`), `valueQuantity` (in `Observation`) |

: Anatomy of a FHIR Resource [@HL7_FHIR_R4]. {#tbl-resource-anatomy}

The `implicitRules` and `language` fields are omitted from slides as they
are rarely used in an introductory context, but merit attention in this
textbook. `implicitRules` indicates that the resource was constructed
following rules additional to a profile or implementation guide. A system
that does not know those rules cannot safely process the resource. The
`language` field is useful in multilingual contexts — in a system serving
populations with different languages, identifying the language of the
content facilitates rendering and translation.

### Type Hierarchy: `Resource` → `DomainResource`

`FHIR` defines a formal type hierarchy. The base class `Resource`
provides the common technical fields — `id`, `meta`, `implicitRules`,
`language`. Most clinical resources do not inherit directly from
`Resource`, but rather from `DomainResource`, which adds three fundamental
capabilities [@HL7_FHIR_R4]:

- `text` — human-readable `XHTML` narrative; allows any system that does
  not know the specific resource type to nonetheless present a readable
  version of the content
- `contained` — mechanism for embedding resources within others (see
  section 8.4)
- `extension` and `modifierExtension` — extensibility mechanisms (see
  below)

```{=html}
<!--
  Diagram: hierarchy Resource → DomainResource → clinical resources.
  Blue palette (IM).
-->
<div style="overflow-x:auto; margin:1.5rem 0;">
<svg viewBox="0 0 680 260" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"
     style="width:100%;max-width:680px;font-family:sans-serif;">
  <rect width="680" height="260" rx="10" fill="#f5f8fc"/>

  <!-- Resource (top) -->
  <rect x="240" y="18" width="200" height="48" rx="8" fill="#1a3a6b"/>
  <text x="340" y="38" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13"
        font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">Resource</text>
  <text x="340" y="56" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9"
        fill="#b8d0f0">id · meta · implicitRules · language</text>

  <!-- Arrow Resource → DomainResource -->
  <line x1="340" y1="66" x2="340" y2="96" stroke="#2d6abf" stroke-width="2"
        marker-end="url(#arr1)"/>

  <!-- DomainResource -->
  <rect x="190" y="98" width="300" height="50" rx="8" fill="#2d6abf"/>
  <text x="340" y="118" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13"
        font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">DomainResource</text>
  <text x="340" y="136" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9"
        fill="#d0e4ff">text · contained · extension · modifierExtension</text>

  <!-- Arrow DomainResource → clinical resources -->
  <line x1="340" y1="148" x2="340" y2="170" stroke="#2d6abf" stroke-width="2"
        marker-end="url(#arr1)"/>

  <!-- Clinical resources (row) -->
  <rect x="20" y="172" width="110" height="36" rx="6" fill="#4a86d8"/>
  <text x="75" y="195" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10"
        font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">Patient</text>

  <rect x="140" y="172" width="110" height="36" rx="6" fill="#4a86d8"/>
  <text x="195" y="195" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10"
        font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">Observation</text>

  <rect x="260" y="172" width="110" height="36" rx="6" fill="#4a86d8"/>
  <text x="315" y="195" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10"
        font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">Encounter</text>

  <rect x="380" y="172" width="110" height="36" rx="6" fill="#4a86d8"/>
  <text x="435" y="195" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10"
        font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">Practitioner</text>

  <rect x="500" y="172" width="160" height="36" rx="6" fill="#4a86d8"/>
  <text x="580" y="195" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10"
        font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">Condition · Medication…</text>

  <!-- Inheritance lines to resources -->
  <line x1="75" y1="172" x2="340" y2="148" stroke="#4a86d8" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="3"/>
  <line x1="195" y1="172" x2="340" y2="148" stroke="#4a86d8" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="3"/>
  <line x1="315" y1="172" x2="340" y2="148" stroke="#4a86d8" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="3"/>
  <line x1="435" y1="172" x2="340" y2="148" stroke="#4a86d8" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="3"/>
  <line x1="580" y1="172" x2="340" y2="148" stroke="#4a86d8" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="3"/>

  <!-- Exceptions -->
  <rect x="120" y="222" width="440" height="28" rx="5"
        fill="#e8f0fb" stroke="#2d6abf" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="340" y="241" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9"
        fill="#1a3a6b">Do not inherit from DomainResource: Bundle · Binary · Parameters</text>

  <text x="340" y="258" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" fill="#64748b">
    Figure 8.1 — FHIR type hierarchy: Resource → DomainResource → clinical resources
  </text>

  <defs>
    <marker id="arr1" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7"
            refX="6" refY="3.5" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L7,3.5 L0,7 Z" fill="#2d6abf"/></marker>
  </defs>
</svg>
</div>
```

Three resources — `Bundle`, `Binary`, and `Parameters` — do not inherit
from `DomainResource`. These are infrastructure resources with special
semantics: they have no `XHTML` narrative and do not support arbitrary
extensions.

### Extensions: `extension` and `modifierExtension`

The extension mechanism is central to the 80/20 philosophy of `FHIR`.
The *core* of the standard covers the most common 80%; the 20% specific
to each country or institution are added via extension, without breaking
the standard.

- **`extension`** — an additional field that does not exist in the *core*
  model. Example: the `NHS` (National Health Service) patient number in a
  Portuguese `Patient` resource. A system that does not know this extension
  may simply ignore it — the resource remains valid.
- **`modifierExtension`** — identical to the above, but with an explicit
  warning: this extension *alters the meaning* of the resource. A system
  that does not understand it cannot safely ignore it — it must reject the
  resource.

The distinction between the two is important in interoperability contexts:
`modifierExtension` fields signal that something outside the *core* is
semantically determinative.

### Categories of `FHIR` Resources

`FHIR` R4 defines more than 140 resources, organised into categories by
functional domain:

| Category | Representative resources | Role |
|----------|--------------------------|------|
| Clinical | `Patient`, `Observation` (clinical observation), `Condition` (diagnosis) | Information directly related to the patient's clinical state |
| Administrative | `Organization` (healthcare organisation), `Practitioner` (healthcare professional), `Encounter` (clinical encounter) | Context and actors of care |
| Financial | `Claim` (reimbursement request), `Coverage` (health coverage: NHS, subsystems, insurance) | Payment and insurance management |
| Infrastructure | `Bundle` (resource container), `CapabilityStatement` (capability declaration), `OperationOutcome` (operation result) | Operation of the `FHIR` server |

: Categories of FHIR R4 resources and representative examples [@HL7_FHIR_R4]. {#tbl-resource-categories}

::: {.callout-note}
**Note** — `Practitioner` is deliberately broad: it encompasses doctors,
nurses, pharmacists, physiotherapists, radiography technicians, and any
other professional qualified to provide healthcare. Professional category
distinctions are made through associated qualification fields, not through
the resource type itself.
:::

## System ID vs Clinical ID {#sec-fhir-identifiers}

### Two Types of Identifier

A `FHIR` `Resource` may have two types of identifier with completely
distinct semantics. Understanding the difference is essential for correctly
implementing searches and cross-system references.

The **system ID** (also referred to in slides as the *logical ID*) is the
technical identifier assigned by the `FHIR` server to the resource. It
appears in the `id` field of the resource and forms part of the `URL`:

```
GET /Patient/550e8400-e29b-41d4
               └─ this is the system ID
```

The system ID has two important properties:

1. **It is unique within the server** — two different `FHIR` servers may
   have resources with the same system ID without any conflict.
2. **It may change** — if data are migrated to a new server, the system
   ID is typically reassigned. The resource `Patient/123` on a legacy
   system may become `Patient/550e8400-e29b` on the new server.

The **clinical ID** (also referred to in slides as the *business
identifier*) is the identifier that exists in the real world, independent
of the server. It is stored in the `identifier` field of the resource and
persists even through migrations:

```json
"identifier": [{
  "system": "http://rnsnp.min-saude.pt",
  "value": "123456789"
}]
```

The NHS patient number, the medical record number, the `TIN` (Tax
Identification Number) — these are examples of clinical IDs. Healthcare
professionals recognise and use these identifiers in day-to-day practice.
When a clinician requests a patient's data, they do not know the system
ID — they know the patient number.

| Dimension | System ID | Clinical ID |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| Field | `id` | `identifier` |
| Assigned by | `FHIR` server | Clinical system / issuing entity |
| Appears in the `URL` | Yes | No |
| Persists through migrations | No | Yes |
| Recognised by professionals | Rarely | Always |
| Examples | `550e8400-e29b-41d4` | NHS patient number, record number, TIN |

: Comparison between system ID and clinical ID in FHIR [@HL7_FHIR_R4]. {#tbl-id-comparison}

The `identifier` field is an *array* because a person may have multiple
clinical IDs simultaneously: NHS patient number, hospital record number,
health insurance beneficiary number. Each element of the *array* has a
`system` field — the canonical `URI` of the entity that assigned the
identifier — and a `value` field — the value of the identifier in that
system.

```json
{
  "resourceType": "Patient",
  "id": "550e8400-e29b-41d4",
  "identifier": [
    {
      "system": "http://rnsnp.min-saude.pt",
      "value": "123456789"
    },
    {
      "system": "http://hospitalx.pt/processos",
      "value": "PROC-2024-00891"
    }
  ],
  "name": [{ "family": "Silva", "given": ["Maria"] }],
  "birthDate": "1980-06-15"
}
```

In this example, the system `id` is `550e8400-e29b-41d4` and the resource
`URL` is `/Patient/550e8400-e29b-41d4`. The patient number `123456789` and
the record number `PROC-2024-00891` are clinical IDs — they identify the
same person regardless of the server where the data reside.

## References and Embedded Resources {#sec-fhir-references}

### The `Reference` Type

`FHIR` resources rarely exist in isolation. An `Observation` (clinical
observation) belongs to a patient; an `Encounter` (clinical encounter)
involves a `Practitioner` (healthcare professional); a `DiagnosticReport`
(diagnostic report) groups several `Observation` resources. These
relationships are expressed through the `Reference` type — a typed
reference to another resource [@HL7_FHIR_R4].

A `Reference` has three possible forms:

**Relative reference** (the most common) — points to a resource on the
same server:

```json
"subject": {
  "reference": "Patient/p-123",
  "display": "Maria Silva"
}
```

**Absolute reference** — points to a resource on another server
(*cross-server*):

```json
"subject": {
  "reference": "https://server.hospital.pt/fhir/Patient/p-123",
  "display": "Maria Silva"
}
```

**Embedded reference** (*contained*) — points to a resource embedded
within the current resource (see following section):

```json
"subject": {
  "reference": "#p-local"
}
```

The `display` field is optional and serves only for human-readable
presentation — it has no semantic value for automated systems. The
`reference` field is the technical identifier that systems use to resolve
the reference.

### Embedded Resources (`contained`)

In certain situations, a resource needs to reference another resource
that does not exist independently on the server. The `contained` mechanism
allows a resource to be embedded *within* another.

The most frequent scenario in an interoperability context: an external
laboratory sends a result (`Observation`) that references a patient who
does not exist on the laboratory's `FHIR` server — the patient only exists
on the hospital's server. Instead of sending an external reference that
the laboratory cannot resolve, a minimal `Patient` resource is embedded
directly within the `Observation`.

```json
{
  "resourceType": "Observation",
  "id": "obs-glicemia-001",
  "contained": [{
    "resourceType": "Patient",
    "id": "p-local",
    "name": [{ "text": "João Ferreira" }]
  }],
  "subject": {
    "reference": "#p-local"
  },
  "status": "final",
  "code": {
    "coding": [{
      "system": "http://loinc.org",
      "code": "1558-6",
      "display": "Fasting glucose [Moles/vol] Serum"
    }],
    "text": "Fasting glucose"
  },
  "valueQuantity": {
    "value": 6.4,
    "unit": "mmol/L",
    "system": "http://unitsofmeasure.org"
  }
}
```

In this example — *Observation with embedded Patient* — the `Observation`
still has a reference in the `subject` field, but instead of pointing to
`/Patient/p-123` on an external server, it points to `#p-local`, which is
the `id` of the `Patient` embedded in the `contained` field. The `#`
notation signals that the reference is internal to the resource.

The embedded `Patient` has no `URL` of its own and cannot be accessed
directly — it exists only within this `Observation`, for this specific
purpose.

::: {.callout-note}
**Note** — The embedded resource (*contained*) is a convenience solution
for situations where an external reference is not possible. In practice,
it should be avoided wherever possible: it complicates maintenance,
prevents reuse of the referenced resource, and may create inconsistencies
if the same patient appears embedded in different ways across multiple
resources.
:::

## Primitive and Composite Types {#sec-fhir-datatypes}

### Primitive Types

**Primitive types** are the elementary building blocks of information in
`FHIR` — simple scalar values, with no internal structure, that map
directly to `JSON` data types. They are equivalent to the base types of
any programming language [@HL7_FHIR_R4].

| `FHIR` type | Description | `JSON` example |
|-------------|-------------|----------------|
| `string` | Unicode text | `"Ana Sofia Ferreira"` |
| `boolean` | True/False | `true` · `false` |
| `integer` | Integer number | `42` · `-5` · `0` |
| `decimal` | Decimal number | `6.4` · `98.6` · `3.14` |
| `dateTime` | Date and time `ISO 8601` (International Organization for Standardization 8601 — standard for date and time representation) | `"2025-04-15T09:30:00+01:00"` |
| `date` | Date (without time) | `"2025-04-15"` |
| `uri` / `url` | `URI`/`URL` reference | `"http://loinc.org"` |
| `code` | Vocabulary code | `"active"` · `"male"` · `"final"` |
| `id` | System identifier | `"550e8400-e29b-41d4"` |

: FHIR primitive types and JSON examples [@HL7_FHIR_R4]. {#tbl-primitive-types}

`FHIR` primitive types map directly to `JSON` without additional
*wrapping* layers. A `birthDate` field is a `string` in `JSON`; an
`active` field is a `boolean`; a `multipleBirthInteger` field is an
`integer`. This direct correspondence is one of the reasons why `FHIR`
is easier to implement than `HL7` v3, where data types were structured
and far more verbose.

### Composite Types

**Composite types** are structures with internal fields, composed of
multiple primitives (and possibly other composites). The term "composite"
simply means that they have internal structure — not that they are
complicated [@HL7_FHIR_R4].

The composite types most frequently encountered in a clinical context:

**`HumanName`** — a person's name, with fields for family name, given
name(s), prefix, and suffix:

```json
"name": [{
  "use": "official",
  "family": "Silva",
  "given": ["Maria", "José"],
  "prefix": ["Dr."]
}]
```

**`Address`** — a postal address with fields for street line, city,
postal code, and country:

```json
"address": [{
  "use": "home",
  "line": ["Rua do Comércio, 45"],
  "city": "Vila Real",
  "postalCode": "5000-678",
  "country": "PT"
}]
```

**`Period`** — a time interval with start and end:

```json
"period": {
  "start": "2025-01-10T08:00:00+00:00",
  "end": "2025-01-10T09:30:00+00:00"
}
```

**`Quantity`** — a numerical value with unit and unit system:

```json
"valueQuantity": {
  "value": 6.4,
  "unit": "mmol/L",
  "system": "http://unitsofmeasure.org",
  "code": "mmol/L"
}
```

### Cardinality

**Cardinality** defines how many times each element may occur in a
resource. It is expressed in the notation `minimum..maximum`, where `*`
means "no limit":

| Notation | Meaning | Example |
|----------|---------|---------|
| `0..1` | Optional, at most one occurrence | `Patient.birthDate` — may be absent |
| `0..*` | Optional, multiple occurrences (*array*) | `Patient.name` — may have several names |
| `1..1` | Mandatory, exactly one occurrence | `Observation.status` — always present |
| `1..*` | Mandatory, at least one occurrence | `Bundle.entry` — at least one entry |

: FHIR cardinality notation [@HL7_FHIR_R4]. {#tbl-cardinality}

Cardinality is verifiable by `FHIR` validators. A resource with a
mandatory field absent (`1..1`) or with a singular field repeated (`0..1`
with two values) is invalid. This formal validatability is one of the
advantages of `FHIR` over `HL7` v2, where cardinality was a recommendation
without automatic verification.

The following example shows a `Patient` with composite types and various
cardinalities annotated in comments:

```json
{
  "resourceType": "Patient",
  "id": "p-12345",
  "name": [{
    "use": "official",
    "family": "Silva",
    "given": ["Maria", "José"]
  }],
  "birthDate": "1980-06-15",
  "address": [{
    "use": "home",
    "city": "Porto",
    "country": "PT"
  }]
}
```

In the `name` field (cardinality `0..*`), the array may have zero or more
entries. The composite type `HumanName` contains the fields `family` and
`given`, where `given` is itself an *array* of `string` (cardinality
`0..*`). The `birthDate` field (cardinality `0..1`) is optional but, when
present, is singular. The `address` field (cardinality `0..*`) is an
*array* of `Address`.

## Coding and Terminologies {#sec-fhir-coding}

### The `CodeableConcept` Type

`FHIR` uses the composite type `CodeableConcept` to represent concepts
with a formal code. The need for this type arises from a structural
problem: the same clinical concept exists in multiple terminologies.
*Diabetes mellitus* type 2 is code `73211009` in `SNOMED CT` (Systematized
Nomenclature of Medicine Clinical Terms) and `E11` in `ICD-10`
(International Classification of Diseases, 10th revision). A simple code
presupposes that sender and receiver use the same terminology.
`CodeableConcept` is resilient — it carries the same concept in multiple
terminologies simultaneously, and the receiver uses whichever it knows
[@HL7_FHIR_R4].

The structure of a `CodeableConcept` has two components:

- **`coding`** — *array* of formal codings. Each element is of type
  `Coding`, with three fields: `system` (the canonical `URI` of the
  terminology), `code` (the code in the terminology) and `display` (the
  human-readable designation in that terminology).
- **`text`** — free human-readable text. Preserves the local terminology
  of the originating system. Useful when no formal code is available.

```json
"code": {
  "coding": [
    {
      "system": "http://loinc.org",
      "code": "1558-6",
      "display": "Fasting glucose [Moles/vol] Serum"
    },
    {
      "system": "http://snomed.info/sct",
      "code": "87522002",
      "display": "Fasting blood glucose"
    }
  ],
  "text": "Fasting glucose"
}
```

In this example — the code of an `Observation` for fasting glucose — the
same concept is represented in `LOINC` and in `SNOMED CT` simultaneously.
A system that only knows `LOINC` uses the first `Coding`; a system that
only knows `SNOMED CT` uses the second. The `text` field preserves the
local designation.

The canonical `URI`s of the most common terminologies in a clinical
context:

| Terminology | Canonical `URI` |
|-------------|-----------------|
| `LOINC` | `http://loinc.org` |
| `SNOMED CT` | `http://snomed.info/sct` |
| `ICD-10` | `http://hl7.org/fhir/sid/icd-10` |
| `ATC` (Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical — classification of medicinal products) | `http://www.whocc.no/atc` |

: Canonical URIs of the most common terminologies in FHIR [@HL7_FHIR_R4]. {#tbl-terminology-uris}

### *Binding Strength* — the Degree of Binding to Terminologies

`FHIR` defines four levels of obligation (*binding strength*) for the
binding between resource fields and *ValueSets* (sets of valid values in
a terminology) [@HL7_FHIR_R4]:

**`required`** — mandatory. The code *must* come from the specified
`ValueSet`. If it does not, validation fails. Example: `Patient.gender`
accepts only `"male"`, `"female"`, `"other"`, or `"unknown"`.

**`extensible`** — preferred. If an appropriate code exists in the
`ValueSet`, it *must* be used. Only if the concept is not represented in
the `ValueSet` may a code from another system be used. Example: medical
specialty — the `ValueSet` covers established specialties, but an emerging
specialty may not yet be represented, and `extensible` permits using
another code in such cases.

**`preferred`** — suggested. The recommended `ValueSet` should be used,
but it is not mandatory. Local context may justify different vocabulary
without invalidating the resource. Example: the patient's preferred
language — there is a recommended `ValueSet`, but a hospital may need to
record a regional language or dialect not included in the list.

**`example`** — illustrative only. The `ValueSet` is an example of the
type of values that are appropriate. Any code is acceptable. Used for
very generic elements where the diversity of contexts is too great to
standardise.

| Level | Obligation | Consequence of deviation |
|-------|------------|--------------------------|
| `required` | Mandatory | Validation fails |
| `extensible` | Preferred (use if an appropriate code exists) | Permitted if the concept is not in the `ValueSet` |
| `preferred` | Suggested | Permitted with local justification |
| `example` | Illustrative only | Always permitted |

: Binding strength levels in FHIR [@HL7_FHIR_R4]. {#tbl-binding-strength}

In summary: `required` > `extensible` > `preferred` > `example` — from
most to least restrictive.

## `Bundle` {#sec-fhir-bundle}

### What a `Bundle` Is

A `Bundle` is a container that groups multiple `FHIR` resources into a
single response or transaction. It is the *standard* mechanism for sending
or receiving sets of resources [@HL7_FHIR_R4].

`Bundle` does not inherit from `DomainResource` — it is an infrastructure
resource with its own semantics. Its main field is `type`, which defines
the purpose of the container, and `entry`, which is the *array* of
contained resources.

`FHIR` defines four main types of `Bundle`:

**`searchset`** — the response to a search. When `GET /Patient?family=Silva`
is sent, the server returns a `Bundle` of type `searchset` with the
`Patient` resources found, pagination metadata (total number of results),
and navigation links.

**`transaction`** — a set of operations to be executed atomically. Either
all succeed, or all fail. Used, for example, to create a `Patient`, an
`Encounter`, and an `Observation` simultaneously, guaranteeing that the
operation is atomic — if the creation of the `Encounter` fails, the
`Patient` is not created.

**`message`** — an asynchronous message with a `MessageHeader`. Functions
as the modern replacement for `HL7` v2 messages — *push* of clinical
events to another system.

**`document`** — a clinical document equivalent to `CDA`. The first
`entry` is always a `Composition`; the remaining entries are the resources
referenced in the document.

There is also the **`collection`** type — a collection of resources with
no transactional semantics, used for data exports and archives.

### Structure of a `Bundle searchset`

The most frequent example in `REST` implementations is the `searchset`.
The response to `GET /Patient?family=Silva` is:

```json
{
  "resourceType": "Bundle",
  "type": "searchset",
  "total": 2,
  "link": [{
    "relation": "self",
    "url": "https://fhir.example/Patient?family=Silva"
  }],
  "entry": [
    {
      "fullUrl": "Patient/p-001",
      "resource": {
        "resourceType": "Patient",
        "id": "p-001",
        "name": [{ "family": "Silva", "given": ["João"] }]
      }
    },
    {
      "fullUrl": "Patient/p-002",
      "resource": {
        "resourceType": "Patient",
        "id": "p-002",
        "name": [{ "family": "Silva", "given": ["Maria"] }]
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

The anatomy of this `Bundle searchset`:

- **`type: "searchset"`** — indicates that this is the result of a search
- **`total: 2`** — total number of resources found; may be greater than
  the number of entries returned if the response is paginated
- **`link[]`** — navigation: `"self"` is the current request; if more
  results exist, the server includes `"next"` with the `URL` of the next
  page
- **`entry[]`** — *array* of entries; each entry has `fullUrl` (the
  canonical `URL` of the resource) and `resource` (the complete resource
  in `JSON`)

```{=html}
<!--
  Diagram: anatomy of the Bundle searchset.
  Blue palette (IM).
-->
<div style="overflow-x:auto; margin:1.5rem 0;">
<svg viewBox="0 0 680 240" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"
     style="width:100%;max-width:680px;font-family:sans-serif;">
  <rect width="680" height="240" rx="10" fill="#f5f8fc"/>

  <!-- Title -->
  <text x="340" y="22" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12"
        font-weight="bold" fill="#1a3a6b">Bundle searchset — structure</text>

  <!-- Bundle (envelope) -->
  <rect x="20" y="32" width="640" height="186" rx="8"
        fill="#e8f0fb" stroke="#2d6abf" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="36" y="52" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#1a3a6b">Bundle</text>
  <text x="36" y="68" font-size="9" fill="#2d6abf">type: "searchset"</text>
  <text x="36" y="82" font-size="9" fill="#2d6abf">total: 2</text>
  <text x="36" y="96" font-size="9" fill="#2d6abf">link[]: self / next / previous</text>

  <!-- entry[] label -->
  <text x="36" y="116" font-size="10" font-weight="bold" fill="#1a3a6b">entry[]</text>

  <!-- Entry 1 -->
  <rect x="36" y="124" width="280" height="82" rx="6" fill="#2d6abf"/>
  <text x="52" y="142" font-size="9" fill="#d0e4ff">fullUrl: "Patient/p-001"</text>
  <rect x="52" y="150" width="248" height="48" rx="4" fill="#4a86d8"/>
  <text x="68" y="167" font-size="9" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">resource: Patient</text>
  <text x="68" y="181" font-size="8" fill="#d0e4ff">id: "p-001"</text>
  <text x="68" y="193" font-size="8" fill="#d0e4ff">name: Silva, João</text>

  <!-- Entry 2 -->
  <rect x="364" y="124" width="280" height="82" rx="6" fill="#2d6abf"/>
  <text x="380" y="142" font-size="9" fill="#d0e4ff">fullUrl: "Patient/p-002"</text>
  <rect x="380" y="150" width="248" height="48" rx="4" fill="#4a86d8"/>
  <text x="396" y="167" font-size="9" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">resource: Patient</text>
  <text x="396" y="181" font-size="8" fill="#d0e4ff">id: "p-002"</text>
  <text x="396" y="193" font-size="8" fill="#d0e4ff">name: Silva, Maria</text>

  <text x="340" y="232" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" fill="#64748b">
    Figure 8.2 — Anatomy of a Bundle searchset with two Patient resources
  </text>
</svg>
</div>
```

### `Bundle transaction` — Atomic Operations

The `Bundle` of type `transaction` is used when it is necessary to create
or update multiple resources atomically. Each entry includes, in addition
to the resource, a `request` section specifying the `HTTP` operation to
execute:

```json
{
  "resourceType": "Bundle",
  "type": "transaction",
  "entry": [
    {
      "resource": {
        "resourceType": "Patient",
        "name": [{ "family": "Rodrigues", "given": ["Ana"] }]
      },
      "request": {
        "method": "POST",
        "url": "Patient"
      }
    },
    {
      "resource": {
        "resourceType": "Observation",
        "status": "final",
        "subject": { "reference": "Patient/p-novo" },
        "code": {
          "text": "Blood pressure"
        }
      },
      "request": {
        "method": "POST",
        "url": "Observation"
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

The server processes all operations in the `Bundle transaction` as a
single unit: if any one fails, none are persisted. This atomic guarantee
is essential in clinical contexts where data consistency cannot be partial
— for example, creating a clinical encounter and its associated
observations must be all-or-nothing.

## Proposed Exercises {#sec-fhir-exercicios .unnumbered}

**1.** A Portuguese hospital migrates its data from a legacy system to a
new `FHIR` server. After the migration:

(a) Does the system ID of the `Patient` resources remain the same?
    Justify your answer.
(b) Does the NHS patient number of each patient remain the same? In which
    `FHIR` field should it be stored?
(c) An external system that searched for patients by system ID needs to
    update its queries. Which search parameter should it use instead of
    the system ID?

**2.** Analyse the following `JSON` fragment and answer the questions:

```json
{
  "resourceType": "Observation",
  "id": "obs-ta-001",
  "status": "final",
  "code": {
    "coding": [
      { "system": "http://loinc.org", "code": "8480-6",
        "display": "Systolic blood pressure" }
    ],
    "text": "Tensão arterial sistólica"
  },
  "subject": { "reference": "Patient/p-456" },
  "valueQuantity": {
    "value": 138,
    "unit": "mmHg",
    "system": "http://unitsofmeasure.org"
  },
  "effectiveDateTime": "2025-03-10T10:15:00+00:00"
}
```

(a) What is the system ID of this `Observation`?
(b) Which resource is referenced in the `subject` field? How would that
    reference be resolved on a `FHIR` server?
(c) Which composite type does the `code` field use? Identify the
    terminology and code used.
(d) Which composite type does the `valueQuantity` field use? Which field
    indicates the unit system?

**3.** Consider an external laboratory that sends results to a hospital
`FHIR` server. The laboratory knows the patient's name and date of birth,
but does not have access to the hospital's `Patient` server.

(a) Which `FHIR` mechanism allows the laboratory to include minimal patient
    information in the result without requiring an independent `Patient`
    resource?
(b) Write a `JSON` sketch of an `Observation` that uses that mechanism,
    including only the essential fields.
(c) What limitation does this approach have with regard to
    interoperability?

**4.** A field in a `FHIR` resource has `extensible` *binding* to a
`ValueSet` of medical specialties. The hospital intends to use the
internally defined code `"Hyperbaric-Medicine"` because that specialty
is not yet included in the official `ValueSet`.

(a) Is it valid to use that internal code? Justify your answer based on
    the binding level.
(b) If the field had `required` *binding*, would the answer be different?
(c) Which `FHIR` structure allows including, simultaneously, a code from
    a local terminology and a code from the recommended `ValueSet`?

**5.** A `FHIR` server returns the following `Bundle` in response to
`GET /Observation?patient=p-789&_count=1`:

```json
{
  "resourceType": "Bundle",
  "type": "searchset",
  "total": 47,
  "link": [
    { "relation": "self",
      "url": "https://fhir.hospital.pt/Observation?patient=p-789&_count=1" },
    { "relation": "next",
      "url": "https://fhir.hospital.pt/Observation?patient=p-789&_count=1&_page=2" }
  ],
  "entry": [
    {
      "fullUrl": "Observation/obs-001",
      "resource": {
        "resourceType": "Observation",
        "id": "obs-001",
        "status": "final",
        "code": { "text": "Fasting glucose" },
        "valueQuantity": { "value": 5.2, "unit": "mmol/L" }
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

(a) How many `Observation` resources exist in total for this patient?
(b) How many are returned in this response?
(c) How would the second page of results be obtained?
(d) What do the fields `relation: "self"` and `relation: "next"` indicate?

**6.** Explain, based on the concepts covered in this chapter, why `FHIR`
represented a break from `HL7` v3. Organise the answer around three
dimensions: data model, transport, and extensibility.
